We evaluate them.

Oftentimes I'll run pilots just to see like bottom line revenue and things like that.
Because my team is lean, I very much use AI as a personal assistant of sorts that can allow me to do six jobs at one time.
And wear multiple hats.

But as I've developed more, I have been able to really put critical thought behind what I'm putting into it so that it develops the outputs that I'm looking for.

But recently I was creating a board slide because we have a board meeting in like a week and a half.
And you may be familiar when creating slides like that, you have to condense a lot of material into as few slides as possible, but get the story across.
So I use a lot of data in the work that I'm doing and I use dashboards for that data.
So like Looker is a big tool that I use or Amazon QuickSight is another.
So data in a lot of different places is ThredUP's MO and it can be very time consuming to find all the data points that are most relevant.
So essentially what I do is I take my data dashboards as PDFs or anything.
I uploaded it into chat and then I ask it to basically tell me, I stories and I prompt it.
There are certain certain are certain are certain There are certain
Data points that are most valuable to me, like GMV and just different revenue numbers.

And while I want those numbers to be intriguing, I want them to be truthful as well.
So I plug it in there and then I pop it into, you know, once I'm done having my back and forth, I pop it into my slides and manipulate it to be more personal.
But I mean, if I knew how easy this was a year and a half ago when I was building board slides, I would have leaned on it a lot more.

Also, all of these tools are developing a lot of their own AI tools so that you can do data analysis within those own platforms.
They're just not as skilled, I feel like, as the other models are yet.

I feel like that isn't as much storytelling as I need.

Like I need to have some verbal context with them as well.
But to answer your question, like historically, yes, we've been downloading and then putting them all together, but we're working on different ways to have agents be able to do this for us.
One example would be like we use Asana, the like task monitoring tool to be able to do a lot of our stuff.
So we built an agent between myself and my engineers that live in Ukraine so that the requests for them are a bit more streamlined.
And because we operate on different time zones, it's a lot easier for me just to plug in certain materials into ChachiPT or whatever it is, and then it pipes straight to Asana.
So it creates the task in basically like the code or language that it needs to be so that they can deploy the changes.

gets piped from Slack to Asana and then it ends up on my like to-do list.

using image AI to scan brand and all of that stuff, put it into the system, and then it develops a manifest for me so that I can then use those manifests, share with our external vendor, and they can then decide what products they want to take into their warehouses.

so it was like a natural progression into chief of staff.

Like a very clear.
Customer Discovery Framework or ICP or just understanding on like the real problems we were solving and like who we were better fit to serve and who our team was actually easier.

It was easier for our team to serve in terms of like efficiency and capacity and how much time they're spending because money in the door isn't all the same, especially if adds stress to the team and is custom or whatnot.

So I built out our ICP that we now qualify every new client from and so, you know, polled all the key team members who've been working on accounts and who were very close to each one of the clients with a bunch of questions around customer discovery, did a churn analysis on all the churn that we had to really understand what went wrong, why, what was controllable, what wasn't, what was regrettable, what wasn't.

And then at that point I had worked directly with 20 plus clients as well, so I was able to build out my understanding, my anecdotal understanding of what happened and why and then put it all together.
into like this matrix and framework and then converted that into something that sales can like quickly qualify on a point system.
So now like we can have an objective conversation on like, is this client good to serve or not?

weeks to really know if we're set up for success and if they're a client that we want to have.
so we built out, after going through a few reps without it, this eight-week strategy sprint onboarding system that we're now, we've run 40 clients through.
